text:
I’m old enough to remember when the Trump administration eased US sanctions on Iranian oil. 

“In essence, we will be using the Iranian barrels against Tehran to keep the price down,” US Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent said at the time. 

That was 3 weeks ago. Now a blockade.

VIDEO COLUMN: All what you wanted to know about the Strait of Hormuz.

My @Opinion visual op-ed about the world's most critical waterway; it shipping lanes, and why the strait may never return to its pre-war status.

(Originally published April 10th)
 https://t.co/Pg7UBNarHY

Saudi Arabia summoned the Iraqi ambassador in Riyadh, saying that some of the most recent attacks against the kingdom were launched "from Iraqi territory."

(KSA likely means some of the attacks during the night of April 7-8 that targeted Saudi oil infraestructure)

Three questions: 

1) Would the US stop Chinese-flagged vessels going in-and-out the Persian Gulf? And if the tankers don’t stop? 

2) What’s the plan if the Houthis of Yemen blockade the Bab al-Mandeb? 

3) Would the U.S. stop Iranian tankers loading at Jask (outside the SoH)?

Keep in mind that Iran can divert crude production to Jask. 

This won’t be that easy for the US.
Sellside analysts will start publishing reports about how high oil prices have to get to offset 13-15 million b/d of supply outage.

One thing people may not know is that Iran has been holding floating storage in the Gulf of Oman. 

They have prepared for the possibility of a Naval blockade. They have also waited to sell oil. 

Now that the blockade is here, Iran can use the floating storage to sell the oil to get revenue. This will buy them time. 

In the meantime, oil prices are headed for demand destruction. This will now test which side has more resolve. Can the US really stomach prices that create COVID level demand destruction before the Iranian run out of money?

I don’t know, but it’s going to get messy. 

But if you think the Iranians will fold right away, it won’t be that easy.

The other issue now is that the Strait of Hormuz blockade will introduce a possible Houthis involvement in the Bab el-Mandeb Strait, that’s 4.5-5 million b/d of export. 

If Iran loses 1.6-2 and we lose another 4.5-5 on top, global supply outage would reach 17.1 to 18 million b/d.

The Naval blockade fits in the category of escalate to de-escalate. 

It was going to get worse before it gets better. 

The blockade will knock out 1.6-2 million b/d of Iranian crude/condensate exports. 

Oil market breaking point was an inevitable outcome, so I think it’s a good move to just make it dramatically worse first before coming to a resolution. 

From an oil price standpoint, demand destruction is an inevitability now. 

Now we watch all the signals for oil demand destruction to rebalance the market.

The U.S. laid out clear red lines in negotiations with Iran, according to two U.S. officials briefed on the matter. Among them:

Iran must end all uranium enrichment.

The dismantling of all major nuclear enrichment facilities.

The retrieval of highly enriched material.

A fully reopened Strait of Hormuz that is free of tolls.

A broader peace that includes regional allies.

End funding of proxies such as Hezbollah in Lebanon and Yemen’s Houthi rebels.

 https://t.co/t1rroLknYN

Donald Trump’s threats to mount a blockade of the Strait of Hormuz will have no effect on Iran, the country’s officials said.

Mohammad Bagher Ghalibaf, who led Iran’s negotiating delegation in Pakistan, said Iran “will not surrender to threats”, adding that the US president’s warnings have “no effect on the Iranian people”, in comments reported in state media.

 https://t.co/Hn9dQRpW31

Quoted alongside @reziemba in this @WSJ writeup on the implications of Trump’s threatened blockade of Iranian oil exports and what it means for the oil supply—and global economic—outlook. https://t.co/ndEafAkcaG
Brent crude (June futures) coming in hot out of the weekend. https://t.co/2L4Ic7KRSM
*BRENT CRUDE ADVANCES 8% TO $102.60/BARREL AT START OF TRADING https://t.co/1RXUf1ItKx

All eyes on the first Iranian tanker to test Trump’s blockade.

Iran has thus far left most upstream oil production assets and export infrastructure untouched, but that’s Tehran’s next obvious escalatory ramp should the US Navy attack/seize an Iranian tanker.

More restrained than Trump’s blanket Hormuz blockade announced this morning.

CENTCOM: “The blockade will be enforced impartially against vessels of all nations entering or departing Iranian ports and coastal areas, including all Iranian ports on the Arabian Gulf and Gulf of Oman. CENTCOM forces will not impede freedom of navigation for vessels transiting the Strait of Hormuz to and from non-Iranian ports.”

Bunch of people have asked so to answer directly: these Hormuz offset numbers are entirely bunk.

Absolutely fair to be optimistic about the war ending and Hormuz reopening, but it’s fundamentally flawed to say we’re offset the Hormuz supply loss and irresponsible to clam it’s not a serious ongoing crisis.

Bad estimates (the reality below)

🇸🇦 7M: Saudi Reroute
(That’s total East -West pipeline capacity, already had 2-2.5 on the line so remaining “swing” from Gulf to Red Sea is 4.5-5.0 MMbpd)

📈 4.25M: Pre-War Surplus
(We did have a pre-war surplus, but it was closer to 2 MMbpd, and even that remains disputed—I was on the bearish side of the debate)

🇨🇳 2M: China Safe-Passage
(There is no steady cleared safe passage)

🇦🇪 1.5M: UAE ADCOP reroute
(Again, this is the pipe capacity—swing is more like 0.5-0.7)

🇮🇷 1M: Iran Jask Bypass
(This is silly, Jask never demonstrated that capacity but more fundamentally Iran Hormuz flows actually remain higher than that at 1.5+ MMbpd)

🇮🇳 400k: India Safe-Passage
(There is no steady cleared safe passage)

In reality, we have ~13 MMbpd of upstream Gulf production offline, with no sustainable offset—SPRs, etc. are only a temporary help.

Today’s Trump blockade would raise that to more than 15 MMbpd.
It will be back up in short order... May be a good time to load up for AI revolution #OOTT

Trump: "At 10a tomorrow, we have a blockade going into effect — that'll be 10a tomorrow. Other nations are working, so that Iran will not be able to sell oil and that will be very effective." https://t.co/irofDQQ8al
IRGC PressTV: If President Trump moves on the Strait of Hormuz, Iran will respond by taking control of the Bab al-Mandeb Strait
Saudi Arabia has restored the full pumping capacity of its East-West pipeline to 7 million barrels a day, rehabilitating a vital link for oil exports via the Red Sea - Bloomberg
IRAN IRGC SAYS HORMUZ OPEN FOR NON-MILITARY VESSELS: STATE TV
IRAN'S REVOLUTIONARY GUARDS SAY MILITARY VESSELS APPROACHING STRAIT OF HORMUZ WILL BE CONSIDERED A CEASEFIRE BREACH AND WILL BE DEALT WITH STRONGLY

For the first time in seven years, a National Iranian Tanker Company (NITC) VLCC supertanker named FELICITY (9183934) has brought in two million barrels of Iranian crude oil to India following a sanctions waiver issued by the US Office of Foreign Assets Control (OFAC). The importer is Reliance Industries, and their refinery is based in Sikka. Another few million barrels are expected to arrive over the coming week.
“CENTCOM forces will not impede freedom of navigation for vessels transiting the Strait of Hormuz to and from non-Iranian ports.”
This will get tricky as a number of Iran-linked tankers make bogus port calls in Saudi Arabia and Iraq with the help of AIS spoofing. Good luck with that,  CENTCOM.
As per today's satellite imagery, Iran is floating 23 million barrels of crude oil east of this "US Toll" line which Chinese social media drew for fun. Fact remains stranger than fiction.
Correction: It is now 21 million barrels.
